text: A sixty-five-year-old gentleman with a history of prostate cancer following radiation treatment presented with five to six loose bowel movements per day with gross blood. He had no family history of colon cancer or inflammatory bowel disease.

He underwent a colonoscopy which revealed a large mass at the anal verge extending approximately about fifteen centimeters and comprising one-half of the lumen. Pathology revealed a tubulovilous adenoma and he had a normal CEA. An MRI reveals a large rectal polyp with villous projections extending from the anus to the proximal rectum. There is no evidence of lymphadenopathy or local extension.

The patient is positioned in lithotomy with slight Trendelenburg tilt. The surgical team uses a camera arm and two additional robotic arms, positioned around the surgical field. The anus is dilated, and a gel point path transanal access platform is inserted. An 8.5mm camera and 5mm instruments are visible in use.

The surgeon outlines the polyp with cautery, deepens the mucosal defect, and identifies the submucosal plane. The polyp is lifted, and the distal portion is dissected, continuing the dissection in the appropriate plane.

The surgeon continues dissection on the lateral aspects of the polyp, lifting and dissecting it further proximally. The polyp is retracted into the gel point device, and the proximal aspect is divided.

The surgeon closes a large mucosal defect in a transverse fashion using a V-lock suture, with the robotic camera providing clear visualization of the mucosal edges. The increased wrist motion of the robotic instruments facilitates the suture repair.
After closing the mucosal defect, the surgeon inspects the proximal portion of the rectum to confirm that no remnants of the polyp remain and that there is no visible stricture.

The excised polyp was ten centimeters in its largest dimension and pathology revealed a tubulovilous adenoma with high-grade dysplasia. The patient was discharged on postoperative day two following a bowel movement, and his symptoms significantly improved on his follow-up exam.
Robotic transanal excision of large rectal polyps is a reasonable surgical option. Increased wrist range of motion allows for easier dissection of large tumors and suture repairs of large defects.
